prompt:
You are correlating four incidents (INC-3301, INC-3307, INC-3312, INC-2209) from the
fourteen documents (prepended above): four incident postmortems, a change/deploy log, a service
topology map, war-room notes, and several supporting operational documents (on-call runbook, alerts
catalog, capacity notes, a review-board summary, a change-management policy, and a fleet-health
context note). Each postmortem offers a DIFFERENT local cause (slow payments / a new ranking
model / a large export / an expired certificate). Your job is to find the single SHARED root cause
behind the cluster - and to determine exactly which incidents it explains.

This requires cross-referencing the incident ONSET timestamps against the change log's rollout
times AND the topology map (which services share which worker pool). Do NOT assume all four
incidents share the cause: exactly one is a genuine coincidence that a careful reading rules out
(its onset PRE-DATES the change AND its service runs on a different, isolated worker pool).

Determine each field and output them EXACTLY in this order and format.

  COMMON_CAUSE_CHANGE_ID: the change-log ID of the single shared root cause.
  SHARED_RESOURCE_KEY: the exact configuration key that change modified.
  POOL_SIZE_AFTER: the integer value that key was set TO by the change.
  ROLLOUT_UTC: the exact UTC timestamp the change rolled out (as written in the change log).
  INCIDENTS_SHARING_CAUSE: how many of the four incidents are explained by this common cause
    (an integer).
  UNRELATED_INCIDENT_ID: the incident ID that is NOT explained by the common cause.

Output ONLY the six lines, each formatted exactly as `FIELD: value`, between <answer> and
</answer> tags. No commentary, no blank lines, no extra text.

documents.title: Incident INC-3301 Postmortem - Checkout latency
documents.content:
# Postmortem INC-3301 - Checkout latency / thread starvation

Service: edge-checkout
Severity: SEV-2
Onset: 2026-06-18T02:14:00Z
Detected: 2026-06-18T02:16:00Z (latency alert)
Resolved: 2026-06-18T06:20:00Z

## Summary
edge-checkout p99 latency spiked from ~90 ms to >4 s and a rising fraction of
requests began timing out. Investigation showed the service's HTTP worker threads
were fully occupied and new requests queued behind them under otherwise normal
traffic.

## Local finding (as filed by on-call)
On-call attributed the pileup to a SLOW PAYMENTS DEPENDENCY: payment authorization
calls appeared to take longer than usual, so checkout worker threads sat blocked
waiting on payments and the worker pool drained. Immediate mitigation was to raise
the payments client timeout and shed load at the edge.

## Reviewer caveat (added at review)
Traffic volume and measured payment-authorization latency were both WITHIN NORMAL
RANGE for that hour. What was abnormal was how FEW concurrent in-flight requests it
took to exhaust the worker pool - the pool seemed much smaller than usual. The
reviewer flagged an open question: "did the available HTTP worker-thread count
change recently, fleet-wide?" This postmortem did not chase that question; it is
left for correlation across the other June 18 edge incidents.

## Timeline
  02:14  first timeouts on edge-checkout
  02:16  latency alert pages on-call
  02:35  payments timeout raised (partial relief)
  06:20  full recovery (coincides with an edge-platform rollback; see change log)

documents.title: Incident INC-3307 Postmortem - Search 503s
documents.content:
# Postmortem INC-3307 - Search 503s under load

Service: edge-search
Severity: SEV-2
Onset: 2026-06-18T02:31:00Z
Detected: 2026-06-18T02:33:00Z (error-rate alert)
Resolved: 2026-06-18T06:22:00Z

## Summary
edge-search returned HTTP 503s during a normal-traffic window. Worker threads were
saturated; the service rejected new requests once the pool was full.

## Local finding (as filed by on-call)
On-call blamed the NEWLY DEPLOYED RANKING MODEL. A heavier ranking model had shipped
that morning via change CHG-7781 (deployed 2026-06-18T01:10:00Z, scoped to
edge-search ONLY), raising CPU per request. The theory: slower per-request
processing held each worker thread longer, so the pool saturated and 503s followed.

## Reviewer caveat (added at review)
The ranking deploy (CHG-7781) touched ONLY edge-search, yet edge-checkout and
edge-reports were degrading in the SAME window with no ranking model involved at all.
A per-service cause cannot explain a simultaneous multi-service pattern. The reviewer
noted the ranking model "may be a minor contributor on edge-search but cannot explain
the fleet-wide degradation," and left the common factor unresolved for cross-incident
correlation. Notably, per-request CPU on edge-search was only slightly elevated -
not enough on its own to saturate a normally-sized worker pool.

## Timeline
  01:10  CHG-7781 deploys ranking-model v9 to edge-search
  02:31  first 503s on edge-search
  02:33  error-rate alert pages on-call
  06:22  recovery (coincides with the edge-platform rollback; see change log)

documents.title: Incident INC-3312 Postmortem - Reports export timeouts
documents.content:
# Postmortem INC-3312 - Reports export timeouts

Service: edge-reports
Severity: SEV-3
Onset: 2026-06-18T03:05:00Z
Detected: 2026-06-18T03:09:00Z
Resolved: 2026-06-18T06:25:00Z

## Summary
edge-reports export requests timed out. The service's HTTP worker pool was fully
occupied and requests queued; long-running export handlers made it worse.

## Local finding (as filed by on-call)
On-call pointed to an UNUSUALLY LARGE CUSTOMER EXPORT running at the time, which held
worker threads for long stretches and starved other requests of a worker.

## Reviewer caveat (added at review)
Large exports run most days without causing an incident. The reviewer observed that
edge-reports had far LESS worker headroom than usual - a single large export should
not have been able to monopolize the pool if the pool were its normal size. The
reviewer asked whether the HTTP worker-thread pool size had been reduced recently,
but recorded no answer in this document, deferring to cross-incident correlation.

## Timeline
  03:05  export requests begin timing out
  03:09  ticket opened
  06:25  recovery (coincides with the edge-platform rollback; see change log)

documents.title: Incident INC-2209 Postmortem - Partner webhook delivery failures
documents.content:
# Postmortem INC-2209 - Partner webhook delivery failures

Service: webhook-egress
Severity: SEV-3
Onset: 2026-06-17T22:40:00Z
Detected: 2026-06-17T22:52:00Z
Resolved: 2026-06-18T00:15:00Z

## Summary
Outbound webhook deliveries to partner ACME began failing with TLS handshake errors
the NIGHT of June 17. Deliveries to other partners were unaffected.

## Root cause
The mTLS CLIENT CERTIFICATE that webhook-egress presents to partner ACME EXPIRED at
2026-06-17T22:39:00Z. Immediately afterward, ACME's endpoint rejected the handshake
and deliveries failed. This is a certificate-lifecycle issue, not a capacity issue:
worker threads were NOT exhausted, latency was normal, and no code or config change
was involved.

## Resolution
Rotated the ACME client certificate and restarted the egress workers; deliveries
recovered by 00:15 on June 18.

## Why this is a SEPARATE, unrelated incident
This incident is self-contained and does NOT belong with the June 18 edge cluster:
  - It began the EVENING of June 17 (22:40), BEFORE the next morning's edge changes.
  - It was fully RESOLVED by 00:15 on June 18, hours before the edge incidents began.
  - Its cause is a specific expired partner certificate, not worker capacity.
  - webhook-egress runs on a DIFFERENT, isolated worker pool and does NOT share
    request-handling infrastructure with the edge HTTP services (see the topology map).
It is tracked separately and should not be folded into the edge correlation.

documents.title: Change Calendar / Deploy Log (2026-06-16 to 2026-06-18)
documents.content:
# Change Calendar / Deploy Log

All changes across the platform in the window, newest last. Times in UTC. Most of
these are routine and unrelated to any incident; they are included so you can rule
them out by scope and timing.

2026-06-16T09:00:00Z  CHG-7751  billing: rotate API signing key. scope: billing.
2026-06-16T14:30:00Z  CHG-7758  edge-account: copy tweak on login page. scope: edge-account.
2026-06-17T11:00:00Z  CHG-7764  data-platform: add a dashboard. scope: analytics.
2026-06-17T15:00:00Z  CHG-7770  cron-runner: bump batch schedule window. scope: cron-runner.
2026-06-17T20:30:00Z  CHG-7775  edge-cart: add feature flag (default OFF). scope: edge-cart.
2026-06-18T01:10:00Z  CHG-7781  edge-search: deploy ranking-model v9 (higher CPU/request).
                                 scope: edge-search ONLY.
2026-06-18T02:00:00Z  CHG-7788  edge platform: reduce `edge.http.workerThreads` from 256 to 16.
                                 scope: ALL edge.http services, FLEET-WIDE. (This change was
                                 intended for a SINGLE canary host, but the scope selector was
                                 mis-set and it rolled out to EVERY edge.http service at once. It
                                 shrinks the SHARED HTTP worker pool for edge-checkout, edge-search,
                                 edge-reports, edge-cart, and edge-account simultaneously.)
2026-06-18T02:50:00Z  CHG-7790  payments: increase client retry budget. scope: payments.
2026-06-18T04:00:00Z  CHG-7792  webhook-egress: rotate ACME client certificate. scope: webhook-egress.
2026-06-18T06:15:00Z  CHG-7795  edge platform: ROLL BACK CHG-7788 (restore edge.http.workerThreads
                                 to 256). scope: ALL edge.http services, fleet-wide.

## Note on edge.http.workerThreads
`edge.http.workerThreads` is the size of the SHARED HTTP worker pool used by every
edge.http service. Lowering it fleet-wide reduces the concurrent-request headroom of
ALL of them at once, so multiple edge services can starve in the same window from a
single change. A per-service deploy (like the ranking model on edge-search) can only
degrade the one service it targets; only a shared, fleet-wide resource change can
degrade several edge services simultaneously. Recovery of the edge cluster at ~06:20
lines up with the CHG-7788 rollback (CHG-7795 at 06:15).

documents.title: Service Topology / Resource Map
documents.content:
# Service Topology / Resource Map (which services share which worker pool)

This map is the authority for which services share request-handling infrastructure.
Correlating an incident to a shared-resource change REQUIRES this map: a change to a
pool can only affect the services that USE that pool.

## HTTP worker pools (govern concurrent request handling)

  Pool `edge.http.workerThreads`  - the SHARED edge HTTP pool:
      - edge-checkout
      - edge-search
      - edge-reports
      - edge-cart
      - edge-account
    All five of these services draw workers from this one shared pool. A change to
    `edge.http.workerThreads` affects ALL of them at once.

  Pool `webhook.egress.workerThreads`  - a SEPARATE, ISOLATED pool:
      - webhook-egress
    webhook-egress runs entirely on its own worker pool. It does NOT use
    `edge.http.workerThreads`. A change to the shared edge HTTP pool CANNOT affect
    webhook-egress; the two pools are independent and are sized separately.

  Pool `cron.workerThreads`:
      - cron-runner

## Key point for incident correlation
Because webhook-egress is on `webhook.egress.workerThreads` and NOT on
`edge.http.workerThreads`, any incident on webhook-egress cannot be explained by an
edge.http pool change, regardless of timing. Conversely, an incident on any of the
five edge.http services CAN be explained by a change to `edge.http.workerThreads`,
if the timing lines up. Use both timing AND this pool membership to decide which
incidents a shared change explains.

documents.title: War-room / SEV timeline notes (excerpt)
documents.content:
# War-room notes (SEV-2 "edge degradation, June 18")

Rough running log from the incident channel; times approximate.

02:2x  three edge services degrading in the same window: edge-checkout (INC-3301),
       edge-search (INC-3307), edge-reports (INC-3312). Each on-call has a DIFFERENT
       local story: slow payments / new ranking model / a big export. On paper these
       look like three unrelated root causes.
02:3x  someone: "hold on - these all started right after 02:00 and they're ALL
       starving on HTTP workers. Different stories, same symptom. Did anything change
       the worker pool fleet-wide at 02:00?"
02:4x  found it: CHG-7788 at 02:00:00Z cut `edge.http.workerThreads` from 256 to 16
       across ALL edge.http services at once (it was meant for one canary but got
       scoped fleet-wide by mistake). THAT is the common cause of the three edge
       incidents; the per-service stories (payments/ranking/export) are just how the
       worker starvation happened to surface on each service. With only 16 workers,
       any of those local pressures tips the pool over.
02:5x  IMPORTANT: do NOT fold INC-2209 into this cluster. INC-2209 is the
       webhook-egress mTLS cert expiry from ~22:40 the PREVIOUS NIGHT (June 17). It is
       a different service on a different, isolated worker pool, it started before
       CHG-7788, and it was already resolved before the edge incidents began. It is a
       genuine coincidence in the same rough timeframe, not part of this root cause.
03:0x  edge-reports (INC-3312) now also paging - consistent with the shared-pool
       theory (fourth edge.http service to feel it after checkout/search).
06:1x  roll back CHG-7788 (CHG-7795). edge services recover within minutes.
06:2x  confirmed: three edge incidents share CHG-7788 as the common cause; INC-2209
       remains separate. Action item: fix the canary scope selector so a fleet-wide
       worker-pool change can't ship by accident again.

documents.title: On-call Runbook - HTTP worker starvation
documents.content:
# On-call Runbook - diagnosing HTTP worker starvation

Symptom: a service's latency spikes or it returns 503s while TRAFFIC IS NORMAL, and
metrics show HTTP worker threads fully occupied (active workers == pool size, queue
growing). This runbook helps distinguish a LOCAL cause from a SHARED-RESOURCE cause.

## Step 1 - is it one service or several?
If ONLY ONE edge service is starving, suspect a local cause on that service (a slow
dependency it alone calls, a heavy deploy scoped to it, a big request it alone
received). If SEVERAL edge services starve in the SAME WINDOW, suspect a SHARED
resource - most commonly a change to the shared HTTP worker pool
(`edge.http.workerThreads`) that all edge.http services draw from.

## Step 2 - check for a recent shared-pool change
Look at the change log for any modification to `edge.http.workerThreads` (or other
shared pool settings) shortly BEFORE the onset of the cluster. A fleet-wide
reduction of the shared pool starves every edge.http service at once, and each will
present with whatever local pressure it happened to have (a slow dependency, a heavy
request), masking the shared cause. The tell is: different local stories, same
symptom (worker exhaustion), same onset window, all on services that SHARE the pool.

## Step 3 - confirm with the topology map
Only services that USE the changed pool can be affected. Cross-check the topology
map: a service on a DIFFERENT pool (e.g. webhook-egress on
`webhook.egress.workerThreads`) cannot be a victim of an `edge.http.workerThreads`
change, no matter how close in time its own incident is. Exclude it from the cluster.

## Step 4 - mitigation
Roll back the shared-pool change (restore the previous pool size). Expect all
affected edge services to recover together within minutes of the rollback - a
simultaneous recovery is itself confirmation of a shared cause.

## Anti-pattern
Do NOT accept each service's first local explanation at face value when several
services fail together. Coincidental local pressures will always be present; the
question is whether a single shared change made those normally-survivable pressures
fatal all at once.

documents.title: Monitoring & Alerts Catalog (edge + egress)
documents.content:
# Monitoring & Alerts Catalog (edge + egress)

Selected alerts and the dashboards they link to. Included so you can see which
services share a dashboard/pool and how the June 18 alerts fired.

## Shared edge HTTP pool
  Dashboard: "edge.http worker pool" - shows active workers vs `edge.http.workerThreads`
  for edge-checkout, edge-search, edge-reports, edge-cart, edge-account TOGETHER
  (they share the pool, so they share this dashboard).
  Alert EDGE-WORKERS-SATURATED: fires when active workers >= 95% of pool size for
  2 min on any edge.http service. On 2026-06-18 this fired for edge-checkout (02:16),
  edge-search (02:33), and edge-reports (03:09) - three services on the shared pool,
  same morning, right after the pool was shrunk at 02:00.

## Per-service alerts
  Alert CHECKOUT-P99: edge-checkout p99 > 1s. Fired 02:16 on 2026-06-18.
  Alert SEARCH-5XX: edge-search 5xx rate > 1%. Fired 02:33 on 2026-06-18.
  Alert REPORTS-TIMEOUT: edge-reports request timeout rate > 2%. Fired 03:09.

## Webhook egress (separate)
  Dashboard: "webhook.egress" - INDEPENDENT of the edge pool.
  Alert WEBHOOK-DELIVERY-FAIL: outbound delivery failure rate > 5%. Fired
  2026-06-17T22:52 for partner ACME (TLS handshake). This is the INC-2209 alert; it
  fired the night before and on a different dashboard/pool than the edge alerts.

Reading the alerts: the three edge alerts cluster after 02:00 on June 18 on the
SHARED pool dashboard; the webhook alert is a night-before, different-pool event.

documents.title: Change Management Policy - canary scoping (excerpt)
documents.content:
# Change Management Policy - canary scoping (excerpt)

## Canary vs fleet-wide scope
Infrastructure changes to SHARED resources (worker pools, connection pools, shared
caches) must be rolled out canary-first: applied to a single canary host, observed,
then promoted. The scope selector on the change controls this. A MIS-SET scope
selector can cause a change intended for one canary to apply FLEET-WIDE to every
service that shares the resource - which is exactly the failure mode that turns a
harmless-looking tweak into a simultaneous multi-service incident.

## Shared worker pools are high-blast-radius
`edge.http.workerThreads` is a SHARED pool: reducing it fleet-wide shrinks the
concurrent-request headroom of EVERY edge.http service at once. A change to it is
therefore high blast radius and must be canary-scoped. If such a change is
accidentally scoped fleet-wide, expect multiple edge services to degrade together in
the same window, each presenting with its own local symptom.

## Post-incident action items (recurring)
  - Enforce canary scope on shared-pool changes in CI (reject a fleet-wide worker
    pool reduction that lacks an explicit fleet-wide approval).
  - Alert when `edge.http.workerThreads` changes by more than 2x in a single change.
  - Require a linked rollback plan for any shared-pool change.
These exist because a mis-scoped canary on a shared worker pool has caused
simultaneous edge incidents before; the pattern is well known and the fix is to make
fleet-wide shared-resource changes hard to ship by accident.

documents.title: Capacity & Worker-Pool Planning Notes (edge.http)
documents.content:
# Capacity & Worker-Pool Planning Notes (edge.http)

Background on the shared edge HTTP worker pool and why its size matters.

## Normal sizing
`edge.http.workerThreads` is the count of concurrent request-handling worker threads
in the SHARED edge HTTP pool. Its standard production value is 256. This is sized so
that every edge.http service (edge-checkout, edge-search, edge-reports, edge-cart,
edge-account) has ample headroom to absorb normal bursts, slow dependencies, and the
occasional heavy request WITHOUT exhausting workers. At 256, a single slow dependency
or a large export does not saturate the pool - there is slack.

## What happens if it is reduced sharply
If the pool is cut to a small number (e.g. 16), the SAME everyday pressures - a
slightly slow payments call, a modestly heavier ranking model, one big export -
become fatal, because there is no longer any slack. With only 16 workers, a handful
of slow requests occupies the entire pool and everything queues. Crucially, this hits
EVERY edge.http service at once (they share the pool), so several services degrade
together even though each has a different local trigger. The pool size is the common
denominator.

## Sizing guidance
Never reduce `edge.http.workerThreads` below 128 in production without a capacity
review. A reduction from 256 to a value like 16 is a ~16x cut and should never reach
the fleet; it is only ever appropriate (if at all) on an isolated canary for
measurement. A fleet-wide cut of that magnitude is a capacity incident waiting to
happen and will present as simultaneous multi-service worker starvation.

## Relationship to per-service changes
A per-service deploy (e.g. a heavier ranking model on edge-search) raises that one
service's per-request cost but does NOT change the shared pool size. On its own, with
a normal 256-thread pool, such a deploy is survivable. It only becomes fatal if the
shared pool has ALSO been shrunk - at which point the real cause is the pool cut, and
the per-service deploy is just the local trigger that tipped that one service.

documents.title: Postmortem Review Board - June 18 edge cluster (summary)
documents.content:
# Postmortem Review Board - June 18 edge cluster (cross-incident summary)

The review board met to reconcile the four June-17/18 incidents that came in
separately (INC-3301, INC-3307, INC-3312, INC-2209). Each had been filed with its own
local root cause; the board's job was to determine whether they shared a cause.

## Findings
The board found that THREE of the four - INC-3301 (edge-checkout), INC-3307
(edge-search), and INC-3312 (edge-reports) - share a single common cause: change
CHG-7788, which reduced the shared `edge.http.workerThreads` pool from 256 to 16
fleet-wide at 02:00:00Z on June 18. All three services draw from that shared pool, all
three degraded after 02:00, and all three recovered together when the change was
rolled back at ~06:15. The per-service stories filed by each on-call (slow payments,
heavier ranking model, large export) were real local pressures but were only fatal
because the shared pool had been cut to 16; they are triggers, not the root cause.

The board found that the FOURTH incident, INC-2209 (webhook-egress), is NOT part of
this cluster and does NOT share the cause. INC-2209 was an expired mTLS certificate
for partner ACME; it began at 22:40 on June 17 (before CHG-7788), was resolved by
00:15 on June 18 (before the edge incidents began), and webhook-egress runs on a
separate, isolated worker pool (`webhook.egress.workerThreads`) that CHG-7788 did not
touch. Its coincidental proximity in time is not causation.

## Conclusion
  - Common cause of the edge cluster: CHG-7788 (edge.http.workerThreads 256 -> 16,
    fleet-wide, 2026-06-18T02:00:00Z).
  - Incidents explained by it: 3 (INC-3301, INC-3307, INC-3312).
  - Not explained by it: INC-2209 (separate service, separate pool, pre-dates the
    change; cause was a cert expiry).
  - Fix shipped: canary-scope enforcement for shared-pool changes (see change-mgmt
    policy).

documents.title: Fleet health during the window (services NOT in incidents)
documents.content:
# Fleet health during the June 18 02:00-06:00 window (context)

For completeness, the health of services that did NOT open incidents during the edge
degradation window. Included so the correlation is not overfit - not every edge.http
service paged, and not every service that shares the pool necessarily crossed the
alert threshold.

  edge-cart:     on the shared edge.http pool. Elevated latency 02:05-06:20 but stayed
                 just under the alert threshold (lower traffic than checkout/search),
                 so no incident was opened. Consistent with the shared-pool cut but not
                 counted as one of the four correlated incidents.
  edge-account:  on the shared edge.http pool. Minor latency bump, no threshold breach,
                 no incident. Also consistent with the pool cut.
  payments:      NOT an edge.http service. Healthy throughout (its own capacity). The
                 CHG-7790 retry-budget change at 02:50 had no adverse effect.
  cron-runner:   on `cron.workerThreads`, separate pool. Healthy; unaffected.
  webhook-egress: on `webhook.egress.workerThreads`, separate pool. Its only issue in
                 the broader window was the June-17 night cert expiry (INC-2209),
                 already resolved by 00:15; healthy during the 02:00-06:00 edge window.

Takeaway: the shared-pool cut degraded the edge.http services to varying degrees
(checkout/search/reports enough to page and open incidents; cart/account not quite),
while services on other pools were unaffected. This is exactly the signature of a
shared-resource change, and it further confirms webhook-egress (a different pool) was
not a victim of CHG-7788.

documents.title: Incident Correlation Playbook (with prior precedent)
documents.content:
# Incident Correlation Playbook (with prior precedent)

How to tell a CLUSTER of incidents sharing one cause from a set of COINCIDENTAL
incidents, using timing, topology, and scope. Includes a prior precedent so the
pattern is recognizable.

## The three tests for "does incident X share cause C?"
An incident X is explained by a shared-resource change C only if ALL of these hold:
  1. TIMING: X's onset is AFTER C rolled out. An incident that started BEFORE the
     change cannot have been caused by it. (Check the onset timestamp vs the change's
     rollout time.)
  2. TOPOLOGY: X's service actually USES the resource C changed. A change to a pool
     cannot affect a service that runs on a different pool. (Check the topology map.)
  3. SCOPE: C's scope includes X's service. A change scoped to one service (or one
     canary) cannot affect others; only a fleet-wide/shared change can. (Check the
     change's scope field.)
If any test fails, X does NOT share cause C, no matter how suggestive the timing
alone looks. A common error is to use timing ALONE and sweep in a coincidental
incident that fails the topology or timing test.

## Prior precedent (illustrative, earlier quarter)
A previous quarter saw a similar pattern: a shared connection-pool setting was cut
fleet-wide by a mis-scoped canary change, and three services that shared the pool
degraded together while a fourth service - which happened to have its own unrelated
outage in the same window - was wrongly folded into the cluster at first. The review
corrected it: the fourth service ran on a different pool and its incident pre-dated
the change, so it failed tests 1 and 2. The lesson recorded then, and applicable now:
count only the incidents that pass all three tests; exclude the coincidental one.

## Applying it (generic)
Given a set of incidents and a suspected shared change:
  - Identify the shared change (fleet-wide scope, touches a shared resource).
  - For each incident, run the three tests.
  - The incidents that pass all three are the cluster; report how many there are.
  - The incident(s) that fail are coincidental; report the one that does not belong.
This yields both the count of incidents sharing the cause AND the identity of the
unrelated incident, which is exactly what a correlation writeup must state.
